g.text: This is my take on a design that could go very differently
in different interviews. I included a lot of stuff that probably wouldn't
be asked if other things are, so this design encapsulates many "ideas".
For example you gauge with your interviewer on how recent the data must be
to create a recommendation, and the answer might be "we batch them overnight,
so don't worry about that" (or whatever), and we could leave some stuff out.
another thing is that the insides of inference and training clusters are interesting,
but they might be an overkill for a SWE interview. This is a great deep dive in my opinion and
you could probably abstract it, but I'd ask the interviewer he wants me to dive there.
another thing is you might notice there's no illustration of a database with fields
here like other designs or designs that Hello Interview designs. That's because
I don't like the idea and I believe its redundant, but if you want go ahead and use one.
Last important thing I want to point is that to show that you don't have
a single point of failure and the design is somewhat fault tolerant you will have to discuss
these things explicitly. Replication, Rehydration strategy if used Redis as feature store across design,
I would love some feedback, thanks.
- Track user-item interaction data (clicks, views, purchases, ratings, etc).
- Support real-time model inference at low latency.
- Retrain models with some cadence using fresh data.
User: A person interacting with the platform.
Item: A content/product/media/whatever entity that can be recommended.
Interaction: A timestamped user action on an item (click, view, etc).
ModelVersion: A snapshot of a trained recommendation model.
Recommendation: A list of item IDs returned for a user.
**its ok to post userId, trust me - I'm an engineer
Design a recommendation system that aggregates user interaction data,
trains ML models across distributed nodes, and serves recommendations via distributed inference.
